timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — John Exiled to Patmos

sections.header: Verses 1-5 — "The Sealed Scroll and the Lion of Judah"

heb.paragraph: The scroll (biblion) in the right hand of the one on the throne is 'written on both sides and sealed with seven seals' (v. 1). Writing on both sides (opisthographon) indicates that the document is full—nothing more can be added to God's decree. The seven seals indicate perfect security and completeness: the contents are divinely protected and can be disclosed only by one with the authority to break them. Scholarly debate centers on whether this scroll represents: (1) God's redemptive plan for history, (2) the book of destiny (cf. Dan 12:4, 9), (3) a Roman testament or will (sealed documents required authorized opening), or (4) the new covenant. The most satisfying interpretation sees it as God's comprehensive plan for the consummation of history—the decree that encompasses judgment, redemption, and the renewal of all things.

Revelation's designation for Christ is not the common amnos ('lamb,' used in John 1:29, 36) but the diminutive arnion ('little lamb'), which occurs 28 times in Revelation and only once elsewhere in the NT (John 21:15). The diminutive is theologically loaded: it emphasizes the vulnerability, meekness, and sacrificial character of the one who nevertheless possesses 'seven horns' (complete power) and 'seven eyes' (complete knowledge/omniscience, identified as the sevenfold Spirit). The juxtaposition of Lion (v. 5) and Lamb (v. 6) is Revelation's most important Christological move: the Lion conquers precisely by being the slaughtered Lamb. Power is redefined through sacrifice.

refs.note: The elder's announcement—'the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has triumphed'—combines Jacob's blessing on Judah ('you are a lion's cub') with the promise that 'the scepter will not depart from Judah... until he to whom it belongs shall come.' Christ fulfills the ancient tribal blessing as the conquering Lion of Judah.

The title 'Root of David' draws on Isaiah's messianic prophecy of a shoot from the stump of Jesse. In its Isaianic context, this figure brings justice, righteousness, and peace—a new David who rules not by military might but by the Spirit of the Lord. Revelation reinterprets even this through the Lamb's sacrifice.

The Lamb 'looking as if it had been slain' (hōs esphagmenon) resonates with Isaiah's Suffering Servant who 'was led like a lamb to the slaughter.' The perfect participle esphagmenon indicates a completed action with ongoing effects: the Lamb bears the permanent marks of his sacrifice even in his resurrected, exalted state.

Daniel was told to 'seal up the book until the time of the end.' The scroll of Revelation 5 represents the fulfillment of Daniel's sealed prophecy: the time for unsealing has come because the Lion-Lamb has conquered through his death and resurrection.

notes.note: The scroll in God's right hand contains His complete plan for reclaiming creation from sin, Satan, and death. Written on both sides means the decree is full and final. Seven seals indicate it is perfectly secured by divine authority. No ordinary being can open it because no ordinary being has the right to execute God's sovereign plan for history.

The 'mighty angel' proclaiming 'with a loud voice' emphasizes the cosmic scope of the search. Heaven, earth, and under the earth are searched—the entire created order—and no one is found worthy. John's 'weeping' (eklaion, imperfect tense = continued weeping) reflects the tragedy of a world with no mediator, no redeemer, no one to execute God's plan. Without the Lamb, history has no resolution.

The elder's announcement uses two messianic titles: 'the Lion of the tribe of Judah' (Gen 49:9-10) and 'the Root of David' (Isa 11:1, 10). 'Has triumphed' (enikesen, aorist) points to Christ's completed victory at the cross and resurrection. He has already conquered; the scroll's opening merely reveals and executes the victory already accomplished.

John's weeping reveals the proper response to a world without a mediator. If no one can open the scroll, God's purposes remain locked, evil continues unchecked, and creation groans without resolution. The pathos of John's tears teaches us that the need for Christ is not merely theological but existential—without Him, history is an unresolved tragedy.

The elder says 'Lion' but John sees 'Lamb.' This is perhaps the most important interpretive moment in Revelation: the messianic conqueror conquers not by violence but by sacrifice. Every subsequent vision of judgment must be read through this lens. The God who judges the world is the God who first gave Himself for the world. Power and love, sovereignty and sacrifice, are united in the Lamb.

The 'scroll' (biblion) with seven seals has been compared to Roman legal documents. A testamentum (will) was sealed with seven witnesses' seals and could only be opened after the testator's death by authorized parties. If this background is relevant, the scroll represents the 'last will and testament' of God—His inheritance decree for creation, executable only by His authorized heir, the Lamb.

The perfect tense enikesen ('has conquered') refers to Christ's completed work at the cross and in resurrection. The victory that qualifies the Lamb to open the scroll is not future but past—it has already been accomplished. This completed victory is the theological ground for everything that follows in the book.

Beale argues that the scroll represents God's plan of judgment and redemption for all of history, modeled on the sealed books of Daniel (Dan 7:10; 12:4, 9) and the scroll of Ezekiel 2:9-10 (also written on both sides and containing 'words of lament and mourning and woe'). The Ezekiel background is particularly significant: just as Ezekiel was commissioned to eat the scroll and prophesy, so John will be told to eat a scroll in 10:8-11. The Lion/Lamb juxtaposition is the single most important hermeneutical key in Revelation. John hears 'Lion' but sees 'Lamb'—the pattern of 'hearing then seeing something different' recurs throughout the book (e.g., 7:4-9, where John hears 144,000 but sees a great multitude). In every case, the 'seeing' interprets and redefines the 'hearing': messianic conquest is reinterpreted as sacrificial love.

Osborne observes that the dramatic tension of vv. 1-4 is carefully constructed. The 'strong angel' broadcasting the search with 'a loud voice' (v. 2), the comprehensive failure to find anyone worthy (v. 3), and John's prolonged weeping (v. 4) create maximum suspense before the elder's announcement in v. 5. This is not accidental: the reader must feel the impossibility of the situation before experiencing the shock of the solution. The universe has no one capable of executing God's purposes—except the crucified and risen Christ. The point is that apart from the cross, God's plan for history would remain sealed and unexecuted.

hist.context: Chapter 5 continues the throne room vision of chapter 4 but introduces a crisis: a scroll containing God's purposes for history is sealed, and 'no one in heaven or on earth or under the earth' can open it (v. 3). John's weeping (v. 4) expresses the despair of a world whose future remains locked in divine decree without a mediator to execute it. The resolution comes through one of the most dramatic identity revelations in Scripture: John is told to expect a Lion (v. 5, the conquering Messiah of Gen 49:9-10 and Isa 11:1, 10) but turns to see a Lamb 'looking as if it had been slain' (v. 6). This Lion-to-Lamb transformation is the hermeneutical key to the entire book: every expectation of violent messianic conquest is reinterpreted through the lens of sacrificial death and resurrection. The Lamb conquers not by destroying his enemies but by being slain and rising.

sections.header: Verses 6-14 — "The Lamb Takes the Scroll and All Creation Worships"

heb.paragraph: The 'new song' (v. 9) echoes the Psalter's recurring call to 'sing to the Lord a new song' (Pss 33:3; 40:3; 96:1; 98:1; 144:9; 149:1) and Isaiah 42:10. In the OT, new songs celebrate new acts of deliverance. The content of this new song is the most concentrated soteriological statement in Revelation: the Lamb was slain, his blood purchased people from every tribe, language, people, and nation, and he has made them a kingdom and priests who will reign on the earth. Four key terms—slain, purchased, made, reign—articulate the full arc of redemption: sacrifice, acquisition, transformation, and exaltation.

heb.gloss: myriads of myriads, ten thousands of ten thousands
heb.paragraph: The number of angelic worshipers—'ten thousand times ten thousand, and thousands of thousands' (v. 11)—is drawn from Daniel 7:10 and represents an innumerable host. The Greek myrias means 'ten thousand,' the largest number for which Greek had a specific word; 'myriads of myriads' thus means a number beyond human counting. The progressive expansion of worship in chapter 5—from four living creatures to twenty-four elders to myriads of angels to 'every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and on the sea' (v. 13)—traces an ever-widening circle of praise that encompasses all of creation.

refs.note: Daniel's vision of the divine court—'thousands upon thousands attended him; ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him'—provides the numerical template for the angelic host of v. 11. Revelation intensifies Daniel's imagery by adding these innumerable angels to the worship already offered by the living creatures and elders.

The new song declares that the Lamb has made his redeemed people 'a kingdom and priests' (v. 10)—the same covenant formula God established at Sinai. What Israel was called to be as a nation, the multinational church has become through the Lamb's sacrifice. The priestly kingdom motif bookends Revelation (1:6; 5:10; 20:6).

The universal acclamation of the Lamb—'every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth' (v. 13)—parallels Paul's confession that 'at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth.' Both texts envision the cosmic acknowledgment of Christ's lordship.

The 'new song' tradition celebrates God's new acts of salvation. In Isaiah 42:10, the new song accompanies the announcement of the Servant's mission. In Revelation 5:9, the new song celebrates the Lamb's accomplishment of that mission: redemption accomplished through sacrificial death.

notes.note: The Lamb stands 'in the center of the throne' (en mesō tou thronou)—not merely near or before the throne but at its very center. This is a spatial metaphor for ontological equality: the Lamb shares the throne of God. His seven horns represent omnipotence; His seven eyes represent omniscience through the sevenfold Spirit. The marks of slaughter remain on the resurrected Lamb—His sacrifice is eternally present, not merely historically past.

When the Lamb takes the scroll, heaven erupts in worship. The twenty-four elders hold 'golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of God's people.' This extraordinary image reveals that the prayers of suffering saints are not lost or ignored—they are collected, preserved as precious incense, and presented before the throne. The prayers of the persecuted church participate in the unfolding of God's plan for history.

The new song celebrates four aspects of redemption: (1) the Lamb was slain—substitutionary atonement; (2) His blood purchased people from every ethnic group—universal atonement offered to all; (3) He made them a kingdom and priests—transformed identity; (4) they will reign on the earth—eschatological destiny. This is the gospel in four acts.

The worship crescendo moves from the inner circle (creatures and elders, vv. 8-10) to the angelic host (vv. 11-12) to the entire created order (v. 13). The sevenfold doxology of v. 12 ('power, wealth, wisdom, strength, honor, glory, and praise') matches the sevenfold Spirit and the seven seals—complete praise for complete authority. The fourfold doxology of v. 13 ('praise, honor, glory, and power') is directed to both 'him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb'—co-worship of Father and Son, affirming the Lamb's full deity.

The paradox of the slain Lamb standing alive at the center of God's throne is the heart of Christian theology. The cross is not an embarrassing prelude to glory but the very means of conquest. God's power is most fully displayed not in overwhelming force but in sacrificial love. This is foolishness to the world but the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:23-25). The one who takes the scroll—who executes God's plan for all of history—is not a warrior but a sacrifice.

The new song's emphasis on 'every tribe and language and people and nation' demonstrates the universal scope of redemption. The gospel is not the possession of any single culture, ethnicity, or civilization. The Lamb's blood purchases a multinational, multilingual, multiethnic community—a kingdom that transcends and relativizes every earthly kingdom, including Rome.

The participle esphagmenon ('having been slain') is a perfect passive, indicating a completed action with continuing results. The Lamb does not merely bear old wounds; he perpetually displays the evidence of his sacrifice. The sacrificial death is not a past event to be forgotten but a present reality that defines who Christ is eternally.

The textual variant in v. 10 is significant: some manuscripts read 'they will reign' (basileuousin, future) while others read 'they reign' (basileuousin, present). The future reading is slightly better attested and fits the eschatological context: the priestly kingdom will be fully realized in the new creation. The present reading would emphasize inaugurated eschatology—believers already reign in some sense through their union with the enthroned Lamb.

The phrase 'every creature' (pan ktisma) universalizes the worship to include all of creation—the four domains (heaven, earth, under earth, sea) echo Exodus 20:4 and Philippians 2:10. This is the ultimate fulfillment of the creation mandate: all things returning praise to their Creator through the mediating work of the Lamb.

The Lion/Lamb juxtaposition is the hermeneutical key to Revelation's entire Christology. What John hears (Lion, v. 5) is reinterpreted by what he sees (Lamb, v. 6). This pattern of 'hearing and seeing' controls how the reader should interpret every subsequent vision of divine warfare: the Lamb conquers not by destroying enemies but by absorbing their violence and transforming it through resurrection. Beale argues that this hermeneutic requires rethinking the 'battle' scenes of chs. 19-20: the 'sword from his mouth' (19:15) and the 'fire from heaven' (20:9) are not literal military weapons but the powerful word and judgment of the slain-and-risen Lamb.

The new song draws its content from Exodus 19:5-6 (kingdom and priests), Isaiah 53 (slain for others), and Daniel 7:14, 27 (the saints receiving the kingdom). The Lamb's sacrifice creates a new Exodus people: redeemed from slavery (not Egypt but sin), constituted as a priestly kingdom (not at Sinai but through the cross), and destined to reign (not in Canaan but on the renewed earth). Every major OT redemptive event is recapitulated in the Lamb's work.

The expanding circles of worship create a cosmic liturgy that moves centrifugally from the throne to the furthest reaches of creation. This is the eschatological fulfillment of the purpose for which all things were created (4:11): the entire universe returning praise to its Creator and Redeemer. The joint worship of 'him who sits on the throne and the Lamb' (v. 13) is the strongest possible affirmation of binitarian monotheism: two distinct figures receive equal worship without any suggestion that this compromises the oneness of God.

Osborne stresses that the Lamb's paradoxical description—slain yet standing, vulnerable yet all-powerful, a sacrifice yet a sovereign—is the controlling image for the entire book. Every time the reader encounters images of divine power or judgment in the remaining chapters, this Lamb must be kept in mind. The God who judges is the God who was judged; the one who pours out wrath is the one who poured out his blood. This prevents any reading of Revelation's judgment scenes as expressions of divine caprice or vindictiveness. Judgment is exercised by the one who has already demonstrated the depths of divine love at the cross.

The four concentric circles of worship—creatures, elders, angels, all creation—model the missionary expansion of the gospel itself. From the intimate inner circle of those closest to God's presence, worship radiates outward until every creature in every domain joins the chorus. This is the eschatological destiny toward which the church's mission is directed: not merely individual salvation but cosmic restoration, all of creation united in praise of Creator and Redeemer.

hist.context: The Lamb's appearance is paradoxical: he stands 'as if slain' (hōs esphagmenon, v. 6)—bearing the marks of sacrificial death yet alive, standing in the center of the throne itself. His seven horns represent complete power (horns symbolize strength in the OT: Deut 33:17; 1 Sam 2:1, 10; Ps 75:10), and his seven eyes are identified as 'the seven spirits of God sent out into all the earth' (v. 6)—the Holy Spirit in his comprehensive, all-seeing ministry. When the Lamb takes the scroll from the right hand of the one on the throne, the worship response is overwhelming and cascading. It moves outward in concentric circles: the four living creatures and twenty-four elders (v. 8), accompanied by harps and bowls of incense (the prayers of the saints), sing a new song (vv. 9-10); then myriads of angels join (vv. 11-12); and finally 'every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and on the sea' (v. 13) offers a fourfold doxology to both God and the Lamb. The culmination—worship directed equally to the one on the throne and the Lamb—is one of the strongest NT affirmations of Christ's divine status.

rows.label: vv.1-4
rows.text: Crisis: the sealed scroll and the universal search

rows.label: v.5
rows.text: Resolution announced: Lion of Judah has conquered

rows.label: v.6
rows.text: Resolution revealed: the Lamb, slain and standing

rows.label: v.7
rows.text: Climactic act: the Lamb takes the scroll

rows.label: vv.8-10
rows.text: First circle: creatures, elders, harps, incense, and the new song

rows.label: vv.11-12
rows.text: Second circle: myriads of angels and the sevenfold doxology

rows.label: vv.13-14
rows.text: Third circle: every creature and the fourfold doxology

lit.note: Revelation 5 is structured as a dramatic crisis-resolution-worship sequence. The crisis (vv. 1-4) is resolved by the Lion-Lamb identification (vv. 5-7), and the resolution triggers ever-expanding circles of worship (vv. 8-14). The chapter's movement from despair to universal praise enacts the gospel itself: what seemed impossible is accomplished through the Lamb's sacrifice.

themes.note: Revelation 5 reaches the book's highest concentration of worship language. Sovereignty and mercy converge in the Lamb who is both ruler and sacrifice. Mission scores high because the Lamb's redemption explicitly encompasses 'every tribe and language and people and nation'—the most universalistic statement of salvation's scope in Revelation.

hebtext.note: The scroll (biblion) in the right hand of the one on the throne is 'written on both sides and sealed with seven seals' (v. 1). Writing on both sides (opisthographon) indicates that the document is full—nothing more can be added to God's decree. The 'new song' (v. 9) echoes the Psalter's recurring call to 'sing to the Lord a new song' (Pss 33:3; 40:3; 96:1; 98:1; 144:9; 149:1) and Isaiah 42:10. In the OT, new songs celebrate new acts of deliverance. The content of this new song is the most concentrated soteriological statement in Revelation: the Lamb was slain, his blood purchased people from every tribe, language, people, and nation, and he has made them a kingdom and priests who will reign on the earth. Four key terms—slain, purchased, made, reign—articulate the full arc of redemption: sacrifice, acquisition, transformation, and exaltation.

hebtext.gloss: myriads of myriads, ten thousands of ten thousands
hebtext.note: The number of angelic worshipers—'ten thousand times ten thousand, and thousands of thousands' (v. 11)—is drawn from Daniel 7:10 and represents an innumerable host. trans.title: Comparing translations in The Scroll and the Lamb

translations.text: Then I saw a Lamb, looking as if it had been slain, standing at the center of the throne, encircled by the four living creatures and the elders. The Lamb had seven horns and seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God sent out into all the earth.

And between the throne and the four living creatures and among the elders I saw a Lamb standing, as though it had been slain, with seven horns and with seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God sent out into all the earth.

And when he had taken it, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down before the Lamb. Each one had a harp and they were holding golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of God’s people.

And when he had taken the scroll, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down before the Lamb, each holding a harp, and golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of the saints.
